Short-answer; papers provided. The stem plus general engineering reasoning is sufficient and the gold answer’s key claims are anchored in the evidence: (i) low-rate (<1C) self-heating is small (<3°C) enabling treating estimates as true for lookup tables, (ii) at ≥2C self-heating can be large (~14.8°C) so the cell operates at a different temperature than the chamber setpoint, (iii) Ds,n is current-sensitive and temperature-sensitive (unlike εs,n w.r.t. current) and is a sensitive parameter affecting end-of-discharge fidelity, and (iv) the 0.5% voltage error criterion applies to intermediate SOC/low-rate and does not generalize to high-rate/low-SOC where nonlinearities amplify errors. No contradictions with the evidence bundle and no hidden paper-only definitions are required beyond what is explicitly described.
